channel.title: Posts on Kaseeias site
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on Kaseeias site

item.title: La Lingua Italiana 0.0
item.description: 0.1 Unita’ Introduttiva Observate: Singulare o Plurale? English Singolare Plurale Student Student -e Student -i Page Pagin -a Pagin -e Key Chiav -e Chiav -i Icecream Gelat -o Gelat -i Observate: Maschili o Femminili? Maschili (Masculine) Femminili (Feminine) Singolare Plurale Singolare Plurale Gelat -o Gelat -i Pagin -a Pagin -e Student -e Student -i Chiav -e Chiav -i -o: masculine, plural form > -i -a: feminine, plural form > -e -e: Could be both masculine/feminine, plural form > -i Articolo Determinativo (Definite Articles)

item.title: Grammatica Latina 0.2
item.description: 2.0 Cases We’ve mentioned the concept of “cases” in Latin last time, and in this chapter, we’ll have a close look into all these different cases, and also, in this chapter, we’ll read some paragraphs in Latin together for the first time! So, without further ado, let’s get into the topic. Nominative: Nominative is the case for subject. Ego manduco carnem. -> “Ego” is the subject here. Caro manducata est a me.

item.title: Grammatica Latina 0.1
item.description: 1.0 Word Class in Latin There are 8 classes of word in Latin: “noun, pronoun, verb, adverb, adjective, conjunction, interjection, and preposition.” There’s NO ARTICLE (such as, “the” or “a/an” in English) in Latin. Noun, such as Caesar(Emperor), Domus(house), and Canis(dog). Pronoun, such as: Meus(Mine): Tabula est mea. (The tablet is mine) Ille(That): Ille vir. (That man) Qui(Who): Qui fecit hoc? (Who did this?) Adjective, such as: Altus(Tall): Altus homo. (Tall man) Albus(White): Alba domus.

item.title: Grammatica Latina 0.0
item.description: 0.1 Purposes of Learning Latin Language Although it’s an undeniable truth that Latin language itself has now become an heritage of time, men will still say the influence and the importance of Latin are inmeasureable. Latin served not only as the official language of Roman Empire, but also the official language of most of the nations in the Medieval era. Along with the language itself, many great works written in Latin also left for us to read, and to research; for example, Seneca’s Naturales Questions, Iulius Caeser’s Bellum Gallicum, or even the Bible.

date = “2023-08-10” title = “La Lingua Italiana 0.1” 1.1 Prima Unita’ After last course, you shall have learned some basic rules of Italian. Until now, we’ve learned the rules of genders (masculine/feminine), singular/plural forms, definite articles, and “be” verb in Italian. In this chapter, we will learn more about basic sentence structures in Italian. E’ un libro. = (he) is a book. Sono due libri. = (They) are two books.
